mnemonics.title: Thread Creation Steps
mnemonics.content: CREATE: CReate Thread, Execute run(), Alter priority, Set attributes, Terminate gracefully - remember ALL steps for proper thread management.

mnemonics.title: Synchronization Primitives
mnemonics.content: MACS: Mutex (mutual exclusion), Condition variables (wait/signal), Semaphores (counting), Spinlocks (busy-wait) - the FOUR pillars of thread sync.

mnemonics.title: Thread Models
mnemonics.content: MOM: Many-to-One (map MANY users to ONE kernel), One-to-One (1:1 direct), Many-to-Many (M:M multiplexed) - helps recall the three threading models.

acronyms.expansion: POSIX Threads - Portable Operating System Interface for UNIX
acronyms.usage: The standardized thread API used in Linux and UNIX systems for creating and managing threads.
A family of standards specified by the IEEE Computer Society for maintaining compatibility between operating systems.
A set of functions and procedures that application programs use to access operating system services like thread management.
A unique identifier assigned to each thread by the thread library to track and manage individual threads.
A mechanism that allocates storage for each thread, allowing thread-specific data storage.
Mechanisms for processes/threads to communicate; in multithreading, shared memory is often used instead since threads share address space.

visualTips.visualization: Think of a process as a HOUSE (has its own address space) and threads as ROOMS inside the house (share the same house/space). Multiple rooms (threads) can be used simultaneously for different tasks.
Imagine a SINGLE RESTROOM key on a hook. Only one person can hold the key and use the restroom at a time. Others must wait until the key is returned.
Think of a RESTAURANT KITCHEN with multiple chefs (threads). The head chef (scheduler) decides which chef cooks which dish and for how long, switching between tasks efficiently.
Imagine a TAXI STAND with 5 waiting taxis (pooled threads). When a customer arrives, they don't buy a new taxi; they simply use an available one from the stand.

analogies.analogy: User-level threads are like EMPLOYEES in a company office managed by a floor manager (user library). The CEO (kernel) only sees one department. If one employee is busy on a call, the floor manager can switch others. Kernel-level threads are like EMPLOYEES each reporting directly to the CEO - the CEO can see and manage each one individually.
A MUTEX is like a SINGLE-USE PRINTER in an office. Only one person can use it at a time. You must wait in line (block) or keep checking (spin) until it's your turn.
A SEMAPHORE is like a PARKING LOT with a fixed number of spaces. Each car entering decrements the count. When full, new cars must wait. When a car leaves, the count increments and another can enter.

THREAD POOL is like a TEAM OF ASSISTANTS in an office. Instead of hiring and firing an assistant for each task (expensive), you maintain a fixed team. When work arrives, an available assistant takes the job immediately.
